title: What Price Bobby

jokes.text: Bobby reveals he cruises the theater district hoping to meet someone like Nora, then says 'Not only did I meet someone like you, I met you. Now you can't get any more like you than you, can you?'
jokes.setup: Bobby's earnest attempt to impress with his logic
jokes.punchline: The circular, nonsensical reasoning delivered with confidence
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bobby Wheeler moment - his earnest bumbling creates humor through character-specific illogical reasoning

jokes.text: I scored a point, huh? / Yeah, a partial point. / You have a nice smile. / Oh, yeah? You can tell from back there, huh? / Yeah, I'm looking at your eight-by-ten glossy taped to the seat here.
jokes.setup: Bobby thinks she's complimenting his actual smile
jokes.punchline: She's looking at his headshot taped to the seat
jokes.explanation: Well-constructed misdirection that reveals Bobby's desperate promotional tactics

dialogue.speaker: Nora
dialogue.line: You have such a great smile.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Thanks! You really think so?

dialogue.speaker: Nora
dialogue.line: Oh, absolutely. The way it's captured in this eight-by-ten glossy is just perfect.

jokes.text: I'd do something about the mustache and the missing teeth. / Oh, someone must have drawn on the thing again. It's signed, 'As always, Louie.'
jokes.setup: Nora suggests improvements to his headshot
jokes.punchline: Reveals Louie vandalized his professional photo
jokes.explanation: Establishes Louie's petty cruelty while showing Bobby's vulnerability - character-driven humor

dialogue.speaker: Nora
dialogue.line: Bobby, your headshot needs work. You should get a better one taken.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: What's wrong with this one?

dialogue.speaker: Nora
dialogue.line: Well, for starters, someone drew a mustache on it in pen.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: That was Louie!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: It improved it.

jokes.text: That's not pitching. / That's lying.
jokes.setup: Bobby defends his self-promotion about Times review
jokes.punchline: Nora's blunt dismissal
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and delivery - Nora's directness cuts through Bobby's pretension

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I'm just saying, the Times review was very complimentary. I think it's only natural I mention it.

dialogue.speaker: Nora
dialogue.line: Bobby, the Times review said you were 'adequate.'

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Exactly! Very complimentary.

dialogue.speaker: Nora
dialogue.line: That's not complimentary, that's a cry for help.

jokes.text: I hate to be a name-dropper, but once I almost picked up Mike Nichols. / So did I.
jokes.setup: Bobby thinks almost picking up Mike Nichols is impressive
jokes.punchline: Nora casually reveals she actually almost picked him up too
jokes.explanation: Shows the gap between Bobby's and Nora's worlds - his big moment is her casual Tuesday

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: You almost picked up Mike Nichols? That's incredible!

dialogue.speaker: Nora
dialogue.line: Well, I almost picked him up too.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: You did?

dialogue.speaker: Nora
dialogue.line: Yeah, but then I realized it was just some guy who looked like him.

jokes.text: I like your laugh. / Oh, yeah? / And that's my weakest feature.
jokes.setup: Nora compliments Bobby's laugh
jokes.punchline: Bobby self-deprecatingly calls it his weakest feature
jokes.explanation: Character-appropriate self-deprecation that shows Bobby's insecurity

dialogue.speaker: Nora
dialogue.line: That's a wonderful laugh you have.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Thank you. It's my weakest feature.

jokes.text: It's an experimental play about 12 people stuck in a stalled elevator. It raises an age-old question: which is the greater agony... To be stuck in an elevator or to be watching a show about being stuck in an elevator?
jokes.setup: Bobby describes his experimental play
jokes.punchline: Self-aware joke about how boring the play sounds
jokes.explanation: Excellent self-aware humor that shows Bobby has some comedic intelligence despite his desperation

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I know it sounds boring, but that's the whole point of the play. It's supposed to make you feel nothing.

jokes.text: It will not be all right, damn it. I'll be there.
jokes.setup: Bobby keeps assuming she won't show up, Nora keeps insisting she will
jokes.punchline: Nora's frustration boils over into an almost angry commitment
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Nora's determination overriding Bobby's defeatism

dialogue.speaker: Nora
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, I'll be there! Why do you keep saying I won't show up?

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Because you never do.

dialogue.speaker: Nora
dialogue.line: That's not true! I'm going to be there, and I'm going to be ON TIME. I swear to God, Bobby, I will be there if it kills me!

jokes.text: Alex called and said 'I'm never, ever, ever, never, never coming back.' / You sure he didn't say 'I'm never, ever, ever, never coming back, ha-ha?' You know how Alex likes to kid.
jokes.setup: Bobby reports Alex's emphatic statement about never returning
jokes.punchline: Tony's desperate hope that maybe Alex was joking
jokes.explanation: Shows Tony's optimistic denial in the face of clear evidence

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Alex said he's never coming back here again.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You think he was kidding?

jokes.text: You sure he didn't say 'I'm never, ever, ever, never coming back, ha-ha?' You know how Alex likes to kid.
jokes.setup: Elaine can't accept Alex won't return
jokes.punchline: Desperately hopes the emphatic statement was somehow a joke
jokes.explanation: Shows Elaine's denial in character-appropriate way, though not especially original

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Alex won't return my calls.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: He said, 'I never want to see you again.' He was joking, right? Right?

jokes.text: One day I caught him trying to plant a chicken.
jokes.setup: Jim talking about a guy who farmed when he was loaded
jokes.punchline: The absurd image of someone trying to plant a chicken
jokes.explanation: Perfectly absurd image that's both ridiculous and somehow believable from Jim's hippie commune experience

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: This guy was a farmer when he was loaded. I mean, can you imagine? He's out there trying to plant a chicken.

jokes.text: If she can find it.
jokes.setup: Bobby explaining how a manager will take control of his career
jokes.punchline: Louie's brutal assessment that Bobby doesn't have a career to manage
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie - maximum cruelty with minimum words, perfectly timed

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: A manager will take control of my career and guide me to stardom.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Bobby, you gotta have a career before somebody can manage it.

jokes.text: Which foot was yours? / The one on the far left. / I should have known... The one that was overacting.
jokes.setup: Louie asking about Bobby's athlete's foot commercial appearance
jokes.punchline: Louie claiming even Bobby's foot overacts
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie cruelty - the idea that Bobby's acting is so bad even his foot overacts is brilliantly mean

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Even your foot overacts.

jokes.text: No, he doesn't live here anymore. I do! Get out of here! I live here! I live here!
jokes.setup: Louie answering phone call for Bobby
jokes.punchline: Louie's manic insistence that he lives in the cab company
jokes.explanation: Shows Louie's territorial nature and pathetic life in an over-the-top physical moment

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, he's not here right now. Where am I? I live here!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What do you mean where do I live? I live here! In the cab company!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: My apartment? I don't have an apartment! I live here!

jokes.text: I saw you in your play last night, and everything you said is absolutely true... It stinks.
jokes.setup: Nora starting what sounds like praise
jokes.punchline: Brutal honesty that the play is terrible
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection that establishes Nora's direct, no-nonsense character

dialogue.speaker: Nora
dialogue.line: That was... that was really something. I mean, the effort you put into that play was just... remarkable. You really gave it everything you had out there. I could see how much you cared about every moment of it.

dialogue.speaker: Nora
dialogue.line: It was absolutely terrible.

jokes.text: Well, I'm going to have to think about it.
jokes.setup: Nora offering to represent Bobby
jokes.punchline: Bobby's obviously false claim he needs to think about his dream opportunity
jokes.explanation: Bobby's attempt to play it cool when he's obviously desperate

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Well, I appreciate the offer, but I gotta think about it.

dialogue.speaker: Nora
dialogue.line: Think about it? Bobby, this is your dream!

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I know, I know. But I gotta sleep on it.

jokes.text: Here are the keys to my apartment. See ya.
jokes.setup: Professional meeting about career management
jokes.punchline: Sudden shift to personal/sexual invitation
jokes.explanation: Creates the central conflict of the episode - mixing business with personal inappropriately

jokes.text: It is chess. / Damn! I was going to guess that.
jokes.setup: Someone apparently playing a guessing game
jokes.punchline: Tony's disappointed reaction to missing an obvious answer
jokes.explanation: Simple character moment showing Tony's competitive nature even in trivial matters

jokes.text: Or your clothes or your hair or your skin.
jokes.setup: Tony saying you can't get New York out of your blood once you live there
jokes.punchline: Elaine's addition that it gets in everything including clothes and skin
jokes.explanation: Good escalation joke about New York's gritty reality

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You can't get New York out of your blood once you live there.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: It gets in everything - your clothes, your skin...

jokes.text: Let's ask Alex. / Good idea.
jokes.setup: Discussing how to get Alex back after determining they're a burden to him
jokes.punchline: Immediately falling back into their pattern of asking Alex for advice
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic callback - they immediately demonstrate the exact behavior they just identified as problematic

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: So we're agreed? We gotta get rid of Alex.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, he's been nothing but a burden.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: A real deadweight.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So how do we do it?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I don't know. What do you think we should do?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I have no idea. We should ask Alex.

jokes.text: Then we went to her apartment, we talked some business and then we went to bed.
jokes.setup: Bobby describing his day with his new manager
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of sleeping together, creating uncomfortable silence
jokes.explanation: Creates the central cringe moment - Bobby's casual admission of the inappropriate relationship

jokes.text: Wheeler's sleeping with his manager!
jokes.setup: Bobby asking Louie to keep quiet
jokes.punchline: Louie immediately shouting it to the whole garage
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie behavior - immediately doing exactly what he was asked not to do

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Louie, keep this quiet, okay?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, I won't say nothing.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: HEY EVERYBODY! LISTEN UP!

jokes.text: Not only have you found a manager, you've found a... A great manager!
jokes.setup: Elaine starts to say Bobby found both a manager and a girlfriend/lover
jokes.punchline: Awkwardly redirects to avoid acknowledging the sexual relationship
jokes.explanation: Shows Elaine's discomfort with the sexual situation through verbal fumbling

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Bobby found both a manager and a girlfriend.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I mean, a manager and a... a companion. A manager and a friend who is a woman.

jokes.text: Maybe it was just her way of saying, 'Welcome aboard.'
jokes.setup: Trying to comfort Bobby about the sexual encounter
jokes.punchline: Hilariously inappropriate euphemism for sex
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim line - well-meaning but completely missing the emotional complexity with business-like language

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Look, Bobby, it's not a big deal. It's just a natural thing that happens between two people who care about each other.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: But Jim, I'm worried I did it wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Wrong? Bobby, there's no wrong way to... to park the car in the garage.

jokes.text: I'm glad you've finally figured it out that you're not going to get there on your talent.
jokes.setup: Louie praising Bobby for using his body to advance his career
jokes.punchline: Brutal assessment that Bobby has no talent
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie cruelty disguised as advice - completely in character

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You got a good body, Bobby. You use it right, you can go places in this business.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: You really think so, Louie?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Sure. Because let's face it, kid — you got no talent.

jokes.text: I threw up. During the scene on the stage.
jokes.setup: Bobby describing his terrible audition
jokes.punchline: Revelation that he literally threw up on stage
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy that perfectly captures Bobby's nervous incompetence

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I walked out on stage, completely blanked on my lines. The director was staring at me with this look of disgust. I could feel the sweat pouring down my face. And then... I threw up. Right there on stage.

jokes.text: Well, actually, I did throw up in character. I mean, it's not like I didn't use it. And there's nothing in the script that says old Brick can't throw up.
jokes.setup: Bobby got the part despite throwing up
jokes.punchline: Bobby's attempt to justify his vomiting as a character choice
jokes.explanation: Bobby's delusional self-justification shows his ability to rationalize anything as artistic

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: It was a character choice! The guy's nervous, he's anxious... I was just trying to bring authenticity to the role.

jokes.text: First of all, the room you're pointing to is the kitchen.
jokes.setup: Bobby apparently gesturing toward bedroom while making a point about their relationship
jokes.punchline: Nora's matter-of-fact correction that he's pointing at the kitchen
jokes.explanation: Simple visual gag showing Bobby's disorientation and Nora's practical nature

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: You see, that's the problem with our relationship right there.

dialogue.speaker: Nora
dialogue.line: Bobby, that's the kitchen.

jokes.text: And what was the terrible thing you did?
jokes.setup: Bobby saying he may have slept with her to advance his career
jokes.punchline: Nora's implication that this isn't terrible at all
jokes.explanation: Shows Nora's pragmatic attitude toward mixing business and pleasure

dialogue.speaker: Nora
dialogue.line: Well, if that's what it takes to get ahead in this business, I'm willing to make that sacrifice.

jokes.text: Or bismal?
jokes.setup: Trying to choose between business or personal relationship
jokes.punchline: Bobby's made-up word combining both options
jokes.explanation: Weak wordplay that shows Bobby's nervous attempt at humor in an uncomfortable situation

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Why choose? It's a buisness-ship!

jokes.text: Oh, no, you don't. Because if I said come back, you'd come back in a second. / Try me. / Come back. / Okay.
jokes.setup: Bobby claiming he has his self-respect
jokes.punchline: Immediately proving he doesn't by agreeing to come back
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Bobby's complete lack of self-respect despite his claims

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I have my self-respect.

dialogue.speaker: Nora
dialogue.line: Then you won't come back to work here.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: You're right. I won't.

dialogue.speaker: Nora
dialogue.line: Will you come back?

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Yeah, okay.

jokes.text: But at least I have one thing left: I have my shoes. / Oh, no, you don't. Because if I said, 'Give me your shoes and I'll let you come back,' you'd give me your shoes. / Give me your shoes and I'll let you come back. / Please?
jokes.setup: Bobby claiming he at least has his shoes
jokes.punchline: Nora testing and proving he'd give up even those, Bobby begging to do it
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation that pushes the self-respect theme to absurd extremes while staying true to character

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: At least I still have my shoes.

dialogue.speaker: Nora
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Give me your shoes.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: What? No, come on...

dialogue.speaker: Nora
dialogue.line: You said you have them. Give them to me.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Nora, please don't make me do this...

dialogue.speaker: Nora
dialogue.line: Your shoes. Now.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, here! Take them! Take my shoes! I don't need shoes!

jokes.text: If you don't give me my shoes back I'm going to bust your lamp.
jokes.setup: Bobby has been completely humiliated
jokes.punchline: His pathetic threat to break a lamp if he doesn't get his shoes back
jokes.explanation: Shows Bobby finally finding some small backbone, though still in a pathetic way

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: If I don't get my shoes back, I'm gonna break that lamp!

jokes.text: My shoes are not for sale.
jokes.setup: Bobby getting his shoes back after threatening the lamp
jokes.punchline: His proud declaration about his shoes as if he's achieved some great victory
jokes.explanation: Good ending to the sequence - Bobby finding dignity in the smallest possible victory

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I got my shoes back!

jokes.text: Unfortunately, I ran out of money before I found out just how long.
jokes.setup: Alex talking about how long you can ski and party before it gets old
jokes.punchline: Admitting he only stopped because he ran out of money, not because he got bored
jokes.explanation: Self-aware joke that undercuts the pretension of Alex's vacation being spiritually fulfilling

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You know, you can ski and party for only so long before it gets old.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I stopped because I ran out of money, not because I got bored.

jokes.text: Wheeler, cab 544!
jokes.setup: Bobby's dramatic story about losing everything
jokes.punchline: Louie's mundane work assignment bringing him back to reality
jokes.explanation: Good ending that brings Bobby back to his mundane reality after his brief brush with stardom

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: That's a beautiful story, Bobby. Really beautiful. You know what? I'm gonna make you feel better. Tomorrow morning, I want you to come in early and wax all the cabs.

tmdb_overview: An agent agrees to represent Bobby and expects him to be her lover.
insight: Character-driven episode hits 75.5 without standout zingers — deadpan carries the weight.
